title: Async Messaging: Delivery Guarantee Required
description: Meta-policy pattern that activates when async_messaging is enabled and enforces that messaging_delivery_guarantee is explicitly specified. The delivery guarantee is the foundational decision that determines which implementation pattern is selected downstream: at-most-once activates async-fire-and-forget (no retry, lowest latency/overhead), at-least-once activates queue-consumer-idempotency (retry with deduplication, requires key_value_store), exactly-once requires distributed_transactions: true and a transactional outbox pattern. Without an explicit delivery guarantee, no implementation pattern can be correctly selected and the compiler cannot generate a valid messaging architecture. This pattern also enforces the latency floor imposed by asynchronous broker round-trips (minimum ~50ms p95) and the availability ceiling of typical managed message broker SLAs (99.99%). Specs requiring sub-50ms p95 latency should use synchronous RPC patterns (gRPC, REST) instead of async messaging.

cost.costNotes: Meta-policy pattern with no direct cost. Messaging infrastructure costs are in downstream implementation patterns (async-event-driven, async-fire-and-forget, queue-consumer-idempotency).

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Zero cost — meta-policy pattern only. Messaging broker costs are in async-event-driven, async-event-driven--aws/azure/gcp, async-fire-and-forget, and queue-consumer-idempotency patterns.
provenance.adoptionCost: Zero adoption cost — this pattern auto-activates when async_messaging: true is set and enforces delivery guarantee as a hard compilation requirement.
provenance.licenseCost: No license cost — policy enforcement pattern only.

provides.reasoning: Enforces that messaging_delivery_guarantee is explicitly declared when async_messaging is enabled, and validates that the spec's latency and availability requirements are compatible with async broker infrastructure.

requires.reasoning: Async messaging requires a durable message broker. The specific broker implementation is determined by the downstream async-event-driven pattern selected based on cloud constraint and delivery guarantee.

availability.reason: General architectural enforcement pattern applicable to any async messaging system regardless of broker technology or cloud provider

supports_nfr.reason: Async messaging is compatible with any authentication mechanism; message-level auth is handled by broker IAM policies and the downstream implementation pattern.
Async messaging is compatible with both audited and non-audited environments; broker delivery logs serve as the audit trail.
Message brokers support tenant isolation through topic/queue namespacing, separate broker instances, or IAM-scoped access policies.
Async messaging is compatible with PII workloads when message payloads are encrypted at rest and in transit and broker access is IAM-controlled.
Compatible with GDPR when message brokers support configurable retention windows and message deletion for right-to-be-forgotten requirements.
Compatible with HIPAA when brokers enforce encryption at rest and in transit with appropriate BAA coverage from the cloud provider.
Compatible with CCPA when message retention and deletion policies cover consumer data deletion requests within required timeframes.
Compatible with SOX when broker delivery logs provide an immutable audit trail of message publication and consumption events.

Async messaging is fundamentally eventually consistent and cannot directly satisfy read-your-writes requirements. If needsReadYourWrites: true, compensating patterns (outbox + polling) must be added on top.

Durability depends on delivery guarantee: at-most-once sacrifices durability for throughput and is incompatible with durability/strict: true. at-least-once and exactly-once provide durable delivery. Downstream patterns enforce this constraint.

supports_constraints.reason: Primary activation gate — this pattern only applies when async_messaging is explicitly enabled.
Pattern is cloud-agnostic; managed message brokers exist on all major clouds and on-premises (Kafka, RabbitMQ, ActiveMQ).
Pattern is language-agnostic; message broker client SDKs exist for all major languages.
Async messaging applies to backend and data platforms. Not applicable to library or CLI platforms which do not maintain long-lived broker connections.
Async messaging is independent of caching.
Async messaging is frequently used in AI inference pipelines for job queuing but is not limited to AI workloads.
Message brokers support both single-tenant and multi-tenant architectures via topic/queue namespacing and IAM policies.
Async messaging is used in both real-time event-driven and batch job-queue architectures.

exactly-once delivery requires distributed transaction coordination; at-most-once and at-least-once do not. This meta-policy activates regardless; downstream patterns enforce the distributed_transactions constraint.
Async messaging and real-time streaming are complementary: messaging delivers discrete events, streaming processes continuous data flows. Both can be enabled simultaneously.
Async messaging can trigger vector embedding jobs but is not specific to vector search workloads.
Async messaging is compatible with document stores as the backing persistence layer for event consumers.
Key-value stores are used for message deduplication in at-least-once delivery patterns but async messaging activates regardless of this flag.
Async messaging can trigger graph mutation events but is not specific to graph workloads.
Async messaging is commonly used in time-series ingestion pipelines for buffering high-volume metric or event streams.
Async messaging is compatible with OLTP systems, commonly used for transactional outbox pattern and CDC event publication.
Async messaging is used in OLAP systems for event-driven analytical pipeline triggers and streaming ETL.

requires_constraints.reason: When async_messaging is enabled, messaging_delivery_guarantee must be explicitly set. This choice determines the implementation pattern selected: at-most-once → async-fire-and-forget (simple, no retry, lowest overhead, acceptable message loss), at-least-once → queue-consumer-idempotency (retry with deduplication key, requires key_value_store: true), exactly-once → transactional outbox pattern (requires distributed_transactions: true). Without this field set, no downstream implementation pattern can activate. Set constraints.features.messaging_delivery_guarantee to one of: at-most-once | at-least-once | exactly-once.

requires_nfr.reason: Managed message broker SLAs are bounded at 99.99% (four nines). SQS, Service Bus, and Pub/Sub all guarantee 99.99%. Five-nine availability requires multi-region active-active broker topology beyond standard managed service offerings.

Async broker round-trip (publish → broker persist → consumer poll/push → ack) adds at minimum ~50ms p95 for managed broker services. Systems requiring sub-50ms end-to-end latency should use synchronous RPC (gRPC, REST) instead of async messaging.

Broker tail latency under moderate load typically reaches 200ms p99 due to batching, network jitter, and consumer processing variance. Specs requiring sub-200ms p99 at the messaging layer should use synchronous patterns.
Minimum RTO of 1 minute is achievable with managed broker multi-AZ automatic failover. Sub-minute RTO requires active-active broker topology with pre-warmed consumer connections.
Durable message brokers with persistent storage achieve RPO=0 for at-least-once and exactly-once delivery. at-most-once delivery inherently risks message loss during broker failover.

Managed message brokers (Kafka, SQS, Pub/Sub) support up to 1M msg/s (3.6B/hour) with proper partitioning and consumer scaling. Higher throughput requires specialized streaming infrastructure (Kinesis Data Streams, Kafka with high-throughput configuration).

warn_nfr.reason: Async messaging adds broker overhead, connection management, and operational complexity. At <100 jobs/hour (~0.03 msg/s), synchronous API calls or direct database writes are simpler and more cost-effective.
warn_nfr.message: async-messaging-delivery-guarantee-required: peak_jobs_per_hour is {actual} (<100/hour). At this low message volume, async messaging overhead outweighs benefits—consider synchronous RPC or direct writes instead.

warn_nfr.reason: Async messaging is fundamentally eventually consistent. Satisfying read-your-writes requires compensating patterns (transactional outbox + polling or webhook callbacks), adding significant complexity and latency.
warn_nfr.message: async-messaging-delivery-guarantee-required: spec requires needsReadYourWrites=true, but async messaging is eventually consistent. Compensating patterns (outbox + polling) add complexity and latency—consider synchronous request-reply if immediate consistency is critical.

warn_nfr.reason: Async broker round-trip (publish → persist → consume → ack) adds minimum ~50ms p95 latency. Specs requiring sub-50ms p95 should use synchronous RPC (gRPC, REST) instead of async messaging.
warn_nfr.message: async-messaging-delivery-guarantee-required: spec requires p95 latency <50ms (actual target: {actual}ms), but async broker infrastructure adds minimum ~50ms overhead. Consider synchronous RPC patterns (gRPC, REST) for low-latency requirements.
